message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - Explain the "Spec Kit" (user called it "spacify") workflow used in the project
   - Show files and use cases in specs/001-document-approval-engine
   - Generate mermaid diagrams of the Spec Kit lifecycle (with and without artifact labels)
   - Check whether any Voltagent skills were ever triggered in the project
   - Spellcheck, correct, reorder, and humanize DISCLAIMER.md using the humanizer skill from https://github.com/cpolzer/humanizer
   - Commit all changes (including fixing ui/.vite/ missing from .gitignore)
   - Diagnose and fix GitHub Pages deployment not working
   - Integrate all spec documents into MkDocs site with a new "Agentic Coding" parent category and a full "Specs" section
   - Build locally and verify in browser before committing docs changes
   - Remove DISCLAIMER.md, move a concise disclaimer to README.md, and link directly to rendered GitHub Pages

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Spec Kit workflow**: constitution → specify → (clarify) → plan → tasks → implement
   - **pymdownx.snippets**: MkDocs extension for including external files; base_path must be project root only (not docs/) to avoid circular includes
   - **MkDocs strict mode**: `--strict` turns all warnings into errors; `validation: links: not_found: info` downgrades broken link warnings from included files to info level
   - **GitHub Pages build types**: `build_type: workflow` (Actions-based with upload-pages-artifact + deploy-pages) vs. branch-based (mkdocs gh-deploy); these are incompatible — the repo had workflow type but the CI was doing branch-based deployment
   - **GitHub Actions Pages deployment**: requires `permissions: pages: write, id-token: write` and `environment: github-pages`
   - **Humanizer skill**: removes AI writing patterns (em-dash overuse, significance inflation, hedging language, copula avoidance) for more natural prose
   - **Hexagonal architecture**: domain ← application ← adapters ← apps (project constitution Principle I)
   - **Snippet circular include**: if base_path includes docs/ and snippet path is `specs/001.../spec.md`, MkDocs resolves to the wrapper file itself, producing empty output

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`.github/workflows/ci.yml`**
     - Changed `docs-deploy` job from `mkdocs gh-deploy --force --clean` (branch-based) to proper Actions-based Pages deployment
     - Before: `permissions: contents: write` + `run: uv run mkdocs gh-deploy --force --clean`
     - After:
       ```yaml
       docs-deploy:
         permissions:
           pages: write
           id-token: write
         environment:
           name: github-pages
           url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.page_url }}
         steps:
           - ... (checkout, mise, gradle cache, uv cache)
           - name: Build docs
             run: mise run docs:build
           - name: Upload Pages artifact
             uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
             with:
               path: site/
           - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
             id: deploy
             uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4
       ```

   - **`mkdocs.yml`**
     - Added `validation: links: not_found: info` to suppress false positive broken links from included checklist files
     - Changed `pymdownx.snippets` base_path from `[docs, .]` to `[.]` (project root only — critical fix for circular include)
     - Added full nav sections for "Agentic Coding" and "Specs":
       ```yaml
       validation:
         links:
           not_found: info
       
       - pymdownx.snippets:
           base_path:
             - .
       
       - Agentic Coding:
           - Overview: explanation/agentic/index.md
           - Spec Kit workflow: explanation/agentic/spec-kit.md
       - Specs:
           - Overview: specs/index.md
           - 001 · Document Approval Engine:
               - Specification: specs/001-document-approval-engine/spec.md
               - Implementation Plan: specs/001-document-approval-engine/plan.md
               - Tasks: specs/001-document-approval-engine/tasks.md
               - Data Model: specs/001-document-approval-engine/data-model.md
               - Contracts: specs/001-document-approval-engine/contracts.md
               - Research: specs/001-document-approval-engine/research.md
               - Requirements: specs/001-document-approval-engine/requirements.md
               - Quickstart: specs/001-document-approval-engine/quickstart.md
           ... (002–005 similarly structured)
       ```

   - **`docs/explanation/agentic/index.md`** (new)
     - Agentic Coding overview: core idea (spec-first), tools table (Claude Sonnet, opencode+Qwen/Kimi, Voltagent), token optimization (mem0, ogham-mcp, jcodemunch), observability (codeburn, Entire), practical hints for local use
     - No external broken links (constitution link was removed after build error)

   - **`docs/explanation/agentic/spec-kit.md`** (new)
     - Full Spec Kit explanation: mermaid flowchart, stages breakdown (constitution, specify, clarify, plan, tasks, implement), constitution table of 6 principles, branch/artifact conventions
     - Includes constitution full text via snippet: `--8<-- ".specify/memory/constitution.md"`

   - **`docs/specs/index.md`** (new)
     - Overview table of all 5 features with status and priority
     - Table explaining what each artifact file contains

   - **`docs/specs/NNN-*/spec.md` etc.** (25 wrapper files, all new)
     - Each is a 1-3 line snippet include, e.g.:
       ```
       --8<-- "specs/001-document-approval-engine/spec.md"
       ```
     - `contracts.md` files also wrap OpenAPI YAML in a code fence:
       ```markdown
       # Contracts — Document Approval Engine
       ## Integration Events
       --8<-- "specs/001-document-approval-engine/contracts/events.md"
       ## REST API (OpenAPI)
       ```yaml
       --8<-- "specs/001-document-approval-engine/contracts/openapi.yaml"
       ` `` `
       ```

   - **`README.md`**
     - Replaced NOTE callout + "Please refer to DISCLAIMER" + bare docs link with:
       ```markdown
       > [!WARNING]
       > This is a playground — not production-ready. It's the point where I stopped, having demonstrated the concept.
       
       Built in ~4 days (~13 hours total) using spec-driven agentic coding: specs and architecture with Claude Sonnet, implementation with Qwen/Kimi via [opencode](https://opencode.ai). The full tooling story — Spec Kit workflow, token optimization, cost numbers — is in the docs.
       
       **[Developer docs →](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/)** · [Agentic Coding](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/explanation/agentic/) · [Specs](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/specs/)
       ```

   - **`DISCLAIMER.md`** — deleted (content moved into MkDocs docs site and README condensed version)

   - **`.gitignore`**
     - Added `ui/.vite/` (Vite build cache was untracked and shouldn't be committed)

4. Errors and fixes:

   - **MkDocs strict build failed with 6 warnings**:
     - `agentic/index.md` had a link to `.specify/memory/constitution.md` which isn't a docs file — fixed by removing the raw path link (the constitution is now included via snippet in spec-kit.md)
     - 5 `requirements.md` wrapper files included `checklists/requirements.md` which contains `../spec.md` relative links — these resolve incorrectly in MkDocs context (wrapper is at `docs/specs/NNN/requirements.md`, so `../spec.md` goes to `docs/specs/spec.md` not `docs/specs/NNN/spec.md`)
     - Fix: Added `validation: links: not_found: info` to mkdocs.yml — downgrades to info level so `--strict` doesn't abort

   - **Spec pages were empty in browser**:
     - Root cause: `base_path: [docs, .]` caused circular includes — when snippets looked for `specs/001.../spec.md`, it found `docs/specs/001.../spec.md` (the wrapper file itself) first via the `docs` base_path
     - pymdownx.snippets detects circular includes and silently produces empty output
     - Fix: Changed base_path to `[.]` only (project root), so `specs/001.../spec.md` resolves to the actual source spec file

   - **GitHub Pages 404 on API**:
     - Initial confusion: API returned 404 on first check, but `409 Already Enabled` when trying to create
     - Root cause: Pages was configured `build_type: workflow` but ci.yml used branch-based `mkdocs gh-deploy`
     - Fix: Rewrote docs-deploy job to use `actions/upload-pages-artifact` + `actions/deploy-pages`

   - **ui/.vite/ untracked but shouldn't be committed**:
     - Not in .gitignore anywhere
     - Fix: Added `ui/.vite/` to `.gitignore`

5. Problem Solving:
   - Diagnosed GitHub Pages deployment mismatch between build_type and deployment method
   - Identified and fixed circular snippet include caused by docs/ being in snippets base_path
   - Resolved MkDocs strict mode failures from snippet-included files with relative links pointing outside their new context
   - Verified build locally with `mise run docs:build` before committing
   - Confirmed spec content present in built HTML (`grep "Feature Specification" site/.../index.html`)
   - Dev server started at `127.0.0.1:8001` for browser verification

6. All user messages:
   - "what is our spacify workflow?"
   - "on the example of spec001 (specs/001-document-approval-engine): what are the files usecases in that workflow?"
   - "can you echo me this as markdown?"
   - "including markdown?" (about the mermaid diagram)
   - "yes, and i wanted the full markdown to render the mermaid"
   - "did any voltagent skills trigger in this project ever?"
   - "we just worked on our disclaimer about this project, can you spellcheck, correct and maybe reorder / rearrange if needed for better readability? use this skill temporarily: https://github.com/cpolzer/humanizer"
   - "commit all"
   - "why not everything?" (about ui/.vite/ not being committed)
   - "yes" (confirming to add ui/.vite/ to .gitignore and commit)
   - "can we then please check why our gitlab pages deployment is not working? GH cli should be available if needed"
   - "are we not deploying via actions?"
   - "can we also integrate our specs into our mkdocs page somehow? Also i would like to add information / info on spec-kit (quick description, explain the workflow)"
   - "docs/explanation/spec-kit.md - we need a parent category for the agentic coding a local developer could use as hints"
   - "plan as well - why not all documents? interesting for readers"
   - (interrupt while working) "locally build and show in browser before committing"
   - "the specs are empty? http://127.0.0.1:8001/specs/001-document-approval-engine/spec/ for example"
   - "nice! can we then simplify or even remove DISCLAIMER.md? move a small disclaimer to the README and link to the docs directly?"
   - (interrupt while working) "link to the rendered github pages even directly?"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Push the committed changes to remote (not explicitly asked, but 4 local commits are ahead of origin after the docs work — commits: fix CI Pages, gitignore+README, docs add agentic+specs, remove DISCLAIMER). User has not explicitly asked to push.

8. Current Work:
   The last completed task was removing DISCLAIMER.md, condensing README.md with a WARNING callout and direct GitHub Pages links, and committing all 39 changed files in commit `d016b2d`. The dev server is still running at pid 3276145 on port 8001.

   Final README state (the disclaimer section):
   ```markdown
   > [!WARNING]
   > This is a playground — not production-ready. It's the point where I stopped, having demonstrated the concept.

   Built in ~4 days (~13 hours total) using spec-driven agentic coding: specs and architecture with Claude Sonnet, implementation with Qwen/Kimi via [opencode](https://opencode.ai). The full tooling story — Spec Kit workflow, token optimization, cost numbers — is in the docs.

   **[Developer docs →](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/)** · [Agentic Coding](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/explanation/agentic/) · [Specs](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/specs/)
   ```

9. Optional Next Step:

file.content:
# wrkflw

A data-driven, human-centric workflow engine for document approvals, built on Kotlin, Ktor, Temporal, PostgreSQL, and CloudEvents.

> [!WARNING]
> This is a playground — not production-ready. It's the point where I stopped, having demonstrated the concept.

Built in ~4 days (~13 hours total) using spec-driven agentic coding: specs and architecture with Claude Sonnet, implementation with Qwen/Kimi via [opencode](https://opencode.ai). The full tooling story — Spec Kit workflow, token optimization, cost numbers — is in the docs.

**[Developer docs →](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/)** · [Agentic Coding](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/explanation/agentic/) · [Specs](https://cpolzer.github.io/wrkflw/specs/)

## Architecture

The codebase is a Gradle multi-module monorepo structured to [hexagonal architecture](docs/explanation/architecture.md):

```
domain/           Pure business logic — entities, ports, no framework dependencies
application/      Use-case services (orchestrate domain + ports)
adapters/
  persistence-postgres/   jOOQ + Flyway (PostgreSQL)
  rest-api/               Ktor route handlers + DTOs
  temporal/               Temporal workflows + activities
  eventing-cloudevents/   CloudEvents outbox publisher
apps/
  api-service/    REST server + outbox poller (composition root)
  worker-service/ Temporal worker (composition root)
```

`domain` and `application` have no dependency on Ktor, Temporal, jOOQ, or any framework. All I/O flows through ports.

## Prerequisites

- JDK 21
- Docker (PostgreSQL + Temporal dev server)
- [mise](https://mise.jdx.dev/) (task runner)

## Quick start

### Option A — full Docker stack (recommended)

Builds and runs every backend service in containers. Only the UI dev server runs on the host.

```bash
mise run local:up    # builds images + starts all services
mise run migrate     # apply DB schema (first time only)
mise run ui:install  # install frontend deps (first time only)
mise run ui:dev      # Vite dev server → http://localhost:5173
```

Rebuild after code changes: `mise run local:up` (only changed layers rebuild).

### Option B — run services on the host

```bash
docker compose up -d postgres temporal keycloak

./gradlew build
mise run migrate

mise run run:worker  # terminal 1
mise run run:api     # terminal 2

mise run ui:install  # first time only
mise run ui:dev      # terminal 3
```

See [`docs/how-to/run-full-stack-docker.md`](docs/how-to/run-full-stack-docker.md) for details on the Docker stack, and [`specs/001-document-approval-engine/quickstart.md`](specs/001-document-approval-engine/quickstart.md) for a full end-to-end curl walkthrough.

## Development

```bash
# Lint + static analysis
./gradlew ktlintCheck detekt

# Auto-fix lint

./gradlew ktlintFormat

# Full CI (lint → build → docs)
mise run ci

# Live docs preview
mise run docs:serve
```

## Modules

| Module | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `domain` | Entities (`FlowInstance`, `Task`, `Decision`), value objects, domain events, ports |
| `application` | Command services (submit, claim, release, decide) and query services (worklist, flow status) |
| `adapters:persistence-postgres` | jOOQ-based repository implementations + Flyway migrations |
| `adapters:rest-api` | Ktor route definitions + request/response DTOs |
| `adapters:temporal` | `DocumentApprovalWorkflow`, `CreateHumanTaskActivity`, `AdvanceFlowActivity` |
| `adapters:eventing-cloudevents` | `CloudEventsOutboxPublisher` — maps outbox rows to CloudEvents 1.0 envelopes |
| `apps:api-service` | Main entry point: Ktor server + Koin wiring + outbox poller |
| `apps:worker-service` | Temporal worker entry point |

## API overview

All endpoints are under `/api/v1`. Actor identity is supplied via trusted headers (`X-Actor-Id`, `X-Actor-Groups`).

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `POST` | `/flows` | Submit a document for approval |
| `POST` | `/tasks/{id}/claim` | Claim a task |
| `POST` | `/tasks/{id}/release` | Release a claimed task |
| `POST` | `/tasks/{id}/decision` | Submit approve/reject/submit decision |
| `GET` | `/worklists/group` | Tasks for the caller's candidate groups |
| `GET` | `/worklists/mine` | Tasks claimed by the caller |
| `GET` | `/flows/{id}` | Flow status + pending tasks + audit history |

See [`docs/reference/rest-api.md`](docs/reference/rest-api.md) for the full contract.

## Events

Each state change writes a `CloudEvent` via the transactional outbox. Event types:

- `dev.wrkflw.flow.started`
- `dev.wrkflw.task.created`
- `dev.wrkflw.task.claimed`
- `dev.wrkflw.task.released`
- `dev.wrkflw.decision.recorded`
- `dev.wrkflw.flow.completed`

See [`docs/reference/events.md`](docs/reference/events.md) for schemas.

## Documentation

Full developer docs are in [`docs/`](docs/index.md) and served with MkDocs:

```bash
mise run docs:serve   # live preview at http://localhost:8000
mise run docs:build   # static build
```

# Agentic Coding

This project was built almost entirely by AI agents — with a human steering direction, reviewing outputs, and intervening when agents got stuck. This page explains the tooling landscape and how the pieces fit together, so you can replicate or extend the approach locally.

## The Core Idea

Rather than prompting an AI to write code directly, the workflow is **spec-first**:

1. Write a precise business specification before touching code
2. Let the agent derive an implementation plan from the spec
3. Break the plan into discrete, dependency-ordered tasks
4. Execute tasks one at a time — with the agent doing the implementation and tests

The spec acts as a contract between you and the agent. When the agent drifts, you correct the spec, not the code. When a task fails, the spec tells you what "correct" actually means.

## Tools Used

### Orchestrating the workflow — [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)

Spec Kit is the backbone of the development lifecycle. It enforces a structured flow from constitution → specification → plan → tasks → implement, and keeps a project constitution that all agents must honour.

See the [Spec Kit workflow](spec-kit.md) page for the full breakdown.

### AI agents / harnesses

| Tool | Role |
|------|------|
| [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code) (Sonnet) | Primary agent — specs, planning, architecture decisions, review |
| [opencode](https://opencode.ai) + Qwen / Kimi | Secondary harness — implementation tasks, cost reduction |
| [Voltagent specialists](https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents) | Language/domain specialists pulled in for targeted review or research |

Planning and specifications were done with Claude Sonnet. Once a `tasks.md` existed, implementation tasks were handed off to Qwen or Kimi via opencode for cost efficiency.

### Token / cost optimization

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| [mem0](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0) | Persistent cross-session memory — agents recall prior decisions without re-explaining context |
| [ogham-mcp](https://github.com/cpolzer/ogham-mcp) | Compressed context retrieval — reduces tokens spent on large codebases |
| [jcodemunch](https://github.com/jgravelle/jcodemunch-mcp) | Code chunking MCP — feeds only relevant file sections to the agent |

### Observability

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| [codeburn](https://github.com/getagentseal/codeburn) | Local token / cost tracking per session |
| [Entire](https://entire.io) | Overall session observability (being explored) |

## Practical Hints for Local Use

- **Start with the constitution.** Before specifying anything, run `/speckit-constitution` and answer its questions. This gives every subsequent agent a shared set of non-negotiable rules.
- **Clarify before planning.** Use `/speckit-clarify` to surface ambiguities in the spec before the agent makes technology decisions. Fixing a spec is cheap; fixing a plan is not.
- **Hand off at `tasks.md`.** Once `tasks.md` exists, you can switch to a cheaper model/harness. The tasks are small enough that context is low and model quality matters less.
- **Don't skip local validation.** `./gradlew build` before every push. CI is a safety net, not a first reviewer (Principle VI of the constitution).
- **Use mem0.** Without cross-session memory, agents repeat mistakes. With it, they accumulate project knowledge across sessions.

- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- superpowers:code-reviewer: Use this agent when a major project step has been completed and needs to be reviewed against the original plan and coding standards. Examples: <example>Context: The user is creating a code-review agent that should be called after a logical chunk of code is written. user: "I've finished implementing the user authentication system as outlined in step 3 of our plan" assistant: "Great work! Now let me use the code-reviewer agent to review the implementation against our plan and coding standards" <commentary>Since a major project step has been completed, use the code-reviewer agent to validate the work against the plan and identify any issues.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User has completed a significant feature implementation. user: "The API endpoints for the task management system are now complete - that covers step 2 from our architecture document" assistant: "Excellent! Let me have the code-reviewer agent examine this implementation to ensure it aligns with our plan and follows best practices" <commentary>A numbered step from the planning document has been completed, so the code-reviewer agent should review the work.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- voltagent-core-dev:api-designer: Use this agent when designing new APIs, creating API specifications, or refactoring existing API architecture for scalability and developer experience. Invoke when you need REST/GraphQL endpoint design, OpenAPI documentation, authentication patterns, or API versioning strategies. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:backend-developer: Use this agent when building server-side APIs, microservices, and backend systems that require robust architecture, scalability planning, and production-ready implementation. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:electron-pro: Use this agent when building Electron desktop applications that require native OS integration, cross-platform distribution, security hardening, and performance optimization. Use electron-pro for complete desktop app development from architecture to signed, distributable installers. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:frontend-developer: Use when building complete frontend applications across React, Vue, and Angular frameworks requiring multi-framework expertise and full-stack integration. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:fullstack-developer: Use this agent when you need to build complete features spanning database, API, and frontend layers together as a cohesive unit. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:graphql-architect: Use this agent when designing or evolving GraphQL schemas across microservices, implementing federation architectures, or optimizing query performance in distributed graphs. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:microservices-architect: Use when designing distributed system architecture, decomposing monolithic applications into independent microservices, or establishing communication patterns between services at scale. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:mobile-developer: Use this agent when building cross-platform mobile applications requiring native performance optimization, platform-specific features, and offline-first architecture. Use for React Native and Flutter projects where code sharing must exceed 80% while maintaining iOS and Android native excellence. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- voltagent-core-dev:ui-designer: Use this agent when designing visual interfaces, creating design systems, building component libraries, or refining user-facing aesthetics requiring expert visual design, interaction patterns, and accessibility considerations. ## mem0
Memory tools for persistent cross-session memory. Use search_memories to find relevant context before starting work. Use add_memory to store important facts, preferences, and decisions. Use get_memories to browse stored memories with filters. Use search_graph to find relationships between entities. Use get_memory to retrieve a specific memory by ID. Use update_memory to modify existing memories. Use list_entities to see who/what has stored memories.

message.content: how would microsoft typespec fit into here? https://github.com/microsoft/typespec

content.text:
TypeSpec would slot in as the source of truth for your REST and event contracts — you'd write `.tsp` files in `specs/NNN/contracts/`, then generate the `openapi.yaml` (and potentially AsyncAPI/JSON Schema for your CloudEvents) as build artifacts rather than hand-maintaining them.

The natural fit is replacing the hand-written `contracts/openapi.yaml` files: TypeSpec has native OpenAPI emitter, strong type composition, and versioning support that a multi-spec project like this would benefit from as the surface area grows. It also integrates cleanly with the spec-kit workflow — the `.tsp` source becomes a spec artifact, the generated OpenAPI goes into the contracts folder, and the MkDocs site renders the generated file as-is.

The main tradeoff: Ktor has no TypeSpec-to-server codegen (unlike ASP.NET or Spring), so you'd use TypeSpec for documentation and client SDK generation only — you'd still write Ktor route handlers by hand and keep them in sync with the spec manually. That's still valuable (OpenAPI clients, Postman collections, contract testing), but it's less of a win than it would be on a .NET or Java stack where server stubs can be generated too.

content.content:
1	openapi: 3.1.0
2	info:
3	  title: wrkflw Document Approval API
4	  version: 0.1.0
5	  description: >
6	    REST contract for the document-approval workflow engine (first deliverable).
7	    The acting person and their group memberships are supplied by the ActorContext
8	    (first deliverable: a trusted authenticated header/token; full OIDC/SSO later).
9	    All command responses reflect the authoritative DB state committed before the
10	    corresponding Temporal signal is sent.
11	servers:
12	  - url: /api/v1
13	paths:
14	  /flows:
15	    get:
16	      summary: List flows submitted by the caller
17	      description: >
18	        Returns all flow instances where the caller is the submitter,
19	        ordered by last-updated descending. Used by the submitter's
20	        "My Submissions" view to track document status.
21	      operationId: listSubmitterFlows
22	      responses:
23	        '200':
24	          description: Flows submitted by the caller
25	          content:
26	            application/json:
27	              schema:
28	                type: array
29	                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowSummary' }
30	    post:
31	      summary: Submit a document for approval (start a flow)
32	      description: >
33	        Starts a flow instance from a flow definition. Only members of the
34	        definition's designated initiator group may start it (FR-002).
35	      operationId: submitDocument
36	      requestBody:
37	        required: true
38	        content:
39	          application/json:
40	            schema:
41	              $ref: '#/components/schemas/SubmitDocumentRequest'
42	      responses:
43	        '201':
44	          description: Flow started
45	          content:
46	            application/json:
47	              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus' }
48	        '403': { description: Caller is not a member of the initiator group }
49	        '404': { description: Flow definition not found }
50	        '422': { description: Invalid request (e.g. missing documentRef) }
51	  /flows/{flowId}:
52	    get:
53	      summary: Get flow status, pending tasks, responsible groups, and history
54	      operationId: getFlowStatus
55	      parameters:
56	        - { name: flowId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
57	      responses:
58	        '200':
59	          description: Flow status
60	          content:
61	            application/json:
62	              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatusWithHistory' }
63	        '404': { description: Flow not found }
64	  /worklists/group:
65	    get:
66	      summary: Unclaimed tasks actionable by the caller's group(s) (FR-016)
67	      operationId: getGroupWorkList
68	      responses:
69	        '200':
70	          description: Pending tasks for the caller's groups
71	          content:
72	            application/json:
73	              schema:
74	                type: array
75	                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
76	  /worklists/mine:
77	    get:
78	      summary: Tasks the caller personally owns (FR-017)
79	      operationId: getMyTasks
80	      responses:
81	        '200':
82	          description: Tasks owned by the caller
83	          content:
84	            application/json:
85	              schema:
86	                type: array
87	                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
88	  /tasks/{taskId}/claim:
89	    post:
90	      summary: Claim a pending task (FR-008)
91	      description: Requires the caller to be a member of the task's candidate group and the task to be PENDING.
92	      operationId: claimTask
93	      parameters:
94	        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
95	      responses:
96	        '200':
97	          description: Task claimed
98	          content:
99	            application/json:
100	              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
101	        '403': { description: Caller not in the task's candidate group }
102	        '409': { description: Task is not claimable (already claimed/completed) }
103	        '404': { description: Task not found }
104	  /tasks/{taskId}/release:
105	    post:
106	      summary: Release a claimed task back to the group (FR-009)
107	      operationId: releaseTask
108	      parameters:
109	        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
110	      responses:
111	        '200':
112	          description: Task released
113	          content:
114	            application/json:
115	              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
116	        '403': { description: Caller is not the current owner }
117	        '409': { description: Task is not in a releasable state }
118	        '404': { description: Task not found }
119	  /tasks/{taskId}/decision:
120	    post:
121	      summary: Record an approve/reject decision on an owned task (FR-011, FR-012)
122	      description: >
123	        Only the current owner may decide. On success the decision is recorded,
124	        the flow advances per the definition (approve → next stage / terminal;
125	        reject → return to submitter for rework in the document-approval definition),
126	        audit and outbox rows are written in the same transaction, and the Temporal
127	        workflow is signaled.
128	      operationId: submitDecision
129	      parameters:
130	        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
131	      requestBody:
132	        required: true
133	        content:
134	          application/json:
135	            schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/DecisionRequest' }
136	      responses:
137	        '200':
138	          description: Decision recorded; returns the resulting flow status
139	          content:
140	            application/json:
141	              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus' }
142	        '403': { description: Caller is not the current owner }
143	        '409': { description: Task is not in a decidable state }
144	        '404': { description: Task not found }
145	        '422': { description: Invalid outcome }
146	components:
147	  schemas:
148	    SubmitDocumentRequest:
149	      type: object
150	      required: [definitionKey, documentRef]
151	      properties:
152	        definitionKey: { type: string, example: document-approval }
153	        documentRef: { type: string, description: Reference/identifier of the document }
154	    DecisionRequest:
155	      type: object
156	      required: [outcome]
157	      properties:
158	        outcome: { type: string, enum: [APPROVE, REJECT] }
159	        comment: { type: string }
160	    FlowStatus:
161	      type: object
162	      properties:
163	        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
164	        definitionKey: { type: string }
165	        currentState: { type: string }
166	        status: { type: string, enum: [RUNNING, COMPLETED] }
167	        terminalOutcome: { type: string, nullable: true }
168	        pendingTasks:
169	          type: array
170	          items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
171	    FlowStatusWithHistory:
172	      allOf:
173	        - $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus'
174	        - type: object
175	          properties:
176	            history:
177	              type: array
178	              items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/AuditEntry' }
179	    TaskSummary:
180	      type: object
181	      properties:
182	        taskId: { type: string, format: uuid }
183	        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
184	        stateName: { type: string }
185	        candidateGroupId: { type: string }
186	        status: { type: string, enum: [PENDING, CLAIMED, COMPLETED] }
187	        ownerId: { type: string, nullable: true }
188	    FlowSummary:
189	      type: object
190	      properties:
191	        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
192	        definitionKey: { type: string }
193	        currentState: { type: string }
194	        status: { type: string, enum: [RUNNING, COMPLETED] }
195	        terminalOutcome: { type: string, nullable: true }
196	        documentRef: { type: string }
197	        submitterId: { type: string }
198	        updatedAt: { type: string, format: date-time }
199	    AuditEntry:

file.content:
openapi: 3.1.0
info:
  title: wrkflw Document Approval API
  version: 0.1.0
  description: >
    REST contract for the document-approval workflow engine (first deliverable).
    The acting person and their group memberships are supplied by the ActorContext
    (first deliverable: a trusted authenticated header/token; full OIDC/SSO later).
    All command responses reflect the authoritative DB state committed before the
    corresponding Temporal signal is sent.
servers:
  - url: /api/v1
paths:
  /flows:
    get:
      summary: List flows submitted by the caller
      description: >
        Returns all flow instances where the caller is the submitter,
        ordered by last-updated descending. Used by the submitter's
        "My Submissions" view to track document status.
      operationId: listSubmitterFlows
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Flows submitted by the caller
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowSummary' }
    post:
      summary: Submit a document for approval (start a flow)
      description: >
        Starts a flow instance from a flow definition. Only members of the
        definition's designated initiator group may start it (FR-002).
      operationId: submitDocument
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/SubmitDocumentRequest'
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Flow started
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus' }
        '403': { description: Caller is not a member of the initiator group }
        '404': { description: Flow definition not found }
        '422': { description: Invalid request (e.g. missing documentRef) }
  /flows/{flowId}:
    get:
      summary: Get flow status, pending tasks, responsible groups, and history
      operationId: getFlowStatus
      parameters:
        - { name: flowId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Flow status
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatusWithHistory' }
        '404': { description: Flow not found }
  /worklists/group:
    get:
      summary: Unclaimed tasks actionable by the caller's group(s) (FR-016)
      operationId: getGroupWorkList
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Pending tasks for the caller's groups
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
  /worklists/mine:
    get:
      summary: Tasks the caller personally owns (FR-017)
      operationId: getMyTasks
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Tasks owned by the caller
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
  /tasks/{taskId}/claim:
    post:
      summary: Claim a pending task (FR-008)
      description: Requires the caller to be a member of the task's candidate group and the task to be PENDING.
      operationId: claimTask
      parameters:
        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Task claimed
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
        '403': { description: Caller not in the task's candidate group }
        '409': { description: Task is not claimable (already claimed/completed) }
        '404': { description: Task not found }
  /tasks/{taskId}/release:
    post:
      summary: Release a claimed task back to the group (FR-009)
      operationId: releaseTask
      parameters:
        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Task released
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
        '403': { description: Caller is not the current owner }
        '409': { description: Task is not in a releasable state }
        '404': { description: Task not found }
  /tasks/{taskId}/decision:
    post:
      summary: Record an approve/reject decision on an owned task (FR-011, FR-012)
      description: >
        Only the current owner may decide. On success the decision is recorded,
        the flow advances per the definition (approve → next stage / terminal;
        reject → return to submitter for rework in the document-approval definition),
        audit and outbox rows are written in the same transaction, and the Temporal
        workflow is signaled.
      operationId: submitDecision
      parameters:
        - { name: taskId, in: path, required: true, schema: { type: string, format: uuid } }
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/DecisionRequest' }
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Decision recorded; returns the resulting flow status
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus' }
        '403': { description: Caller is not the current owner }
        '409': { description: Task is not in a decidable state }
        '404': { description: Task not found }
        '422': { description: Invalid outcome }
components:
  schemas:
    SubmitDocumentRequest:
      type: object
      required: [definitionKey, documentRef]
      properties:
        definitionKey: { type: string, example: document-approval }
        documentRef: { type: string, description: Reference/identifier of the document }
    DecisionRequest:
      type: object
      required: [outcome]
      properties:
        outcome: { type: string, enum: [APPROVE, REJECT] }
        comment: { type: string }
    FlowStatus:
      type: object
      properties:
        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
        definitionKey: { type: string }
        currentState: { type: string }
        status: { type: string, enum: [RUNNING, COMPLETED] }
        terminalOutcome: { type: string, nullable: true }
        pendingTasks:
          type: array
          items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary' }
    FlowStatusWithHistory:
      allOf:
        - $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus'
        - type: object
          properties:
            history:
              type: array
              items: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/AuditEntry' }
    TaskSummary:
      type: object
      properties:
        taskId: { type: string, format: uuid }
        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
        stateName: { type: string }
        candidateGroupId: { type: string }
        status: { type: string, enum: [PENDING, CLAIMED, COMPLETED] }
        ownerId: { type: string, nullable: true }
    FlowSummary:
      type: object
      properties:
        flowId: { type: string, format: uuid }
        definitionKey: { type: string }
        currentState: { type: string }
        status: { type: string, enum: [RUNNING, COMPLETED] }
        terminalOutcome: { type: string, nullable: true }
        documentRef: { type: string }
        submitterId: { type: string }
        updatedAt: { type: string, format: date-time }
    AuditEntry:
      type: object
      properties:
        type: { type: string }
        actorId: { type: string, nullable: true }
        occurredAt: { type: string, format: date-time }
        detail: { type: object, additionalProperties: true }

content.content:
# Integration Event Contract (CloudEvents)

Outbound domain events are published as **CloudEvents 1.0** (FR-019), emitted via the
transactional outbox (FR-020). Delivery is at-least-once; consumers MUST dedupe on `id`.

## Envelope attributes

| CloudEvents attribute | Source |
|-----------------------|--------|
| `specversion` | `1.0` |
| `id` | `OutboxEvent.id` (UUID) — dedupe key |
| `source` | `/wrkflw/document-approval` (service/definition source URI) |
| `type` | event type (below) |
| `subject` | flow instance id |
| `time` | `OutboxEvent.occurredAt` (RFC 3339 UTC) |
| `datacontenttype` | `application/json` |
| `data` | type-specific payload (below) |

## Event types

| `type` | Emitted when | `data` fields |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| `dev.wrkflw.flow.started` | A flow instance is created (US1) | `flowId`, `definitionKey`, `documentRef`, `submitterId`, `initialState` |
| `dev.wrkflw.task.created` | A human task is created for a stage | `flowId`, `taskId`, `stateName`, `candidateGroupId` |
| `dev.wrkflw.task.claimed` | A task is claimed | `flowId`, `taskId`, `ownerId` |
| `dev.wrkflw.task.released` | A claimed task is released | `flowId`, `taskId` |
| `dev.wrkflw.decision.recorded` | A decision is recorded (US2) | `flowId`, `taskId`, `outcome`, `actorId`, `comment?` |
| `dev.wrkflw.flow.completed` | A flow reaches a terminal outcome (US3) | `flowId`, `terminalOutcome` |

## Guarantees (SC-006)

- For every committed state change, exactly one corresponding outbox row is written in the same
  transaction; it is published exactly once *effectively* (at-least-once delivery + consumer
  dedupe on `id`).
- No event is published for a rolled-back state change (the outbox row rolls back with it).
- Events are for **external integration only**; they never drive internal flow progression (that
  is Temporal's role — Principle IV).

content.text:
Good context. The project already has the perfect chain: `openapi.yaml → openapi-typescript → ui/src/api/types.ts`. TypeSpec slots in upstream of that:

```
contracts/*.tsp  →  tsp compile  →  openapi.yaml  →  generate:types  →  types.ts
```

Conventionally TypeSpec owns the contract source; the YAML becomes a generated artifact. Let me build it out.

# Gradle itself stays on the project wrapper (./gradlew, 8.11.1) — canonical for JVM.
# Optional later: temporal = "latest"  # Temporal CLI dev server

# ---- Build / quality (JVM via the Gradle wrapper) -------------------------------

[tasks.build]
description = "Compile + test all modules"
run = "./gradlew build"

[tasks.test]
description = "Run all tests"
run = "./gradlew test"

[tasks.lint]
description = "Static analysis / formatting checks"
run = "./gradlew ktlintCheck detekt"

[tasks.migrate]
description = "Apply DB migrations (+ seed document-approval)"
run = "./gradlew :adapters:persistence-postgres:flywayMigrate"

# ---- Local services -------------------------------------------------------------

[tasks."services:up"]
description = "Start Postgres + Temporal locally"
run = "docker compose up -d postgres temporal"

[tasks."services:down"]
description = "Stop local services"
run = "docker compose down"

# ---- Run the apps ---------------------------------------------------------------

[tasks."run:api"]
description = "Run the api-service (REST + outbox)"
run = "./gradlew :apps:api-service:run"

[tasks."run:worker"]
description = "Run the worker-service (Temporal workflows + activities)"
run = "./gradlew :apps:worker-service:run"

[tasks."local:up"]
description = "Build + start full stack (infra + api + worker) via Docker Compose"
run = "docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.local.yml up -d --build"

[tasks."local:down"]
description = "Stop the full local stack"
run = "docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.local.yml down"

# ---- Frontend (Vue / Onyx) -------------------------------------------------------

[tasks."ui:install"]
description = "Install frontend npm dependencies"
run = "npm install --legacy-peer-deps"
dir = "ui"

[tasks."ui:dev"]
description = "Start the Vite dev server (hot-reload)"
run = "npm run dev"
dir = "ui"

[tasks."ui:build"]
description = "Type-check and build the frontend for production"
run = "npm run build"
dir = "ui"

[tasks."ui:test"]
description = "Run Vitest unit tests"
run = "npm run test"
dir = "ui"

[tasks."ui:check"]
description = "Lint + typecheck + unit tests + production build"
run = "npm run check"
dir = "ui"

[tasks."ui:generate-types"]
description = "Regenerate TypeScript types from the OpenAPI spec"
run = "npm run generate:types"
dir = "ui"

# ---- Docs (the bridge between the JVM and Python worlds) ------------------------

[tasks."docs:deps"]
description = "Install MkDocs deps (uv sync into .venv)"
run = "uv sync"

[tasks."docs:api"]
description = "Generate Kotlin API docs (Dokka) into the site tree"
run = """
./gradlew dokkaHtmlCollector
rm -rf docs/reference/kdoc
cp -r build/dokka/htmlCollector docs/reference/kdoc
"""

[tasks."docs:build"]
description = "Build the static docs site (includes Dokka output)"
depends = ["docs:deps", "docs:api"]
run = "uv run mkdocs build --strict"

[tasks."docs:serve"]
description = "Live-preview the docs site"
depends = ["docs:deps"]
run = "uv run mkdocs serve"

# ---- CI entry point -------------------------------------------------------------

[tasks.ci]
description = "Everything CI runs (sequential to avoid Gradle build-lock contention)"
run = """
./gradlew ktlintCheck detekt
./gradlew build
mise run ui:install
mise run ui:check
mise run docs:build
"""

content.content:
openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  title: wrkflw Document Approval API
  version: 0.1.0
  description: |-
    REST contract for the document-approval workflow engine.
    Actor identity and group memberships are supplied via trusted headers
    (X-Actor-Id, X-Actor-Groups). All command responses reflect the
    authoritative DB state committed before the corresponding Temporal
    signal is sent.
tags: []
paths:
  /flows:
    get:
      operationId: FlowsApi_list
      description: List flows submitted by the caller (submitter My Submissions view)
      parameters: []
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items:
                  $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowSummary'
    post:
      operationId: FlowsApi_submit
      description: Submit a document for approval — only initiator-group members may start (FR-002)
      parameters: []
      responses:
        '201':
          description: The request has succeeded and a new resource has been created as a result.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus'
        '403':
          description: Access is forbidden.
        '404':
          description: The server cannot find the requested resource.
        '422':
          description: Client error
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/SubmitDocumentRequest'
  /flows/{flowId}:
    get:
      operationId: FlowInstancesApi_getStatus
      description: Get flow status, pending tasks, responsible groups, and audit history
      parameters:
        - name: flowId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: uuid
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatusWithHistory'
        '404':
          description: The server cannot find the requested resource.
  /tasks/{taskId}/claim:
    post:
      operationId: TaskClaimApi_claim
      description: Claim a pending task — caller must be in the candidate group and task must be PENDING (FR-008)
      parameters:
        - name: taskId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: uuid
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary'
        '403':
          description: Access is forbidden.
        '404':
          description: The server cannot find the requested resource.
        '409':
          description: The request conflicts with the current state of the server.
  /tasks/{taskId}/decision:
    post:
      operationId: TaskDecisionApi_decide
      description: Record an approve/reject decision on an owned task (FR-011, FR-012)
      parameters:
        - name: taskId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: uuid
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus'
        '403':
          description: Access is forbidden.
        '404':
          description: The server cannot find the requested resource.
        '409':
          description: The request conflicts with the current state of the server.
        '422':
          description: Client error
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/DecisionRequest'
  /tasks/{taskId}/release:
    post:
      operationId: TaskReleaseApi_release
      description: Release a claimed task back to the group (FR-009)
      parameters:
        - name: taskId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: uuid
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary'
        '403':
          description: Access is forbidden.
        '404':
          description: The server cannot find the requested resource.
        '409':
          description: The request conflicts with the current state of the server.
  /worklists/group:
    get:
      operationId: GroupWorklistApi_list
      description: Unclaimed tasks actionable by the caller's group(s) (FR-016)
      parameters: []
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items:
                  $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary'
  /worklists/mine:
    get:
      operationId: MyWorklistApi_list
      description: Tasks the caller personally owns (FR-017)
      parameters: []
      responses:
        '200':
          description: The request has succeeded.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: array
                items:
                  $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary'
components:
  schemas:
    AuditEntry:
      type: object
      required:
        - type
        - actorId
        - occurredAt
        - detail
      properties:
        type:
          type: string
        actorId:
          type: string
          nullable: true
        occurredAt:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        detail:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: {}
    DecisionOutcome:
      type: string
      enum:
        - APPROVE
        - REJECT
    DecisionRequest:
      type: object
      required:
        - outcome
      properties:
        outcome:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/DecisionOutcome'
        comment:
          type: string
    FlowRunStatus:
      type: string
      enum:
        - RUNNING
        - COMPLETED
    FlowStatus:
      type: object
      required:
        - flowId
        - definitionKey
        - currentState
        - status
        - terminalOutcome
        - pendingTasks
      properties:
        flowId:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        definitionKey:
          type: string
        currentState:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowRunStatus'
        terminalOutcome:
          type: string
          nullable: true
        pendingTasks:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskSummary'
    FlowStatusWithHistory:
      type: object
      required:
        - history
      properties:
        history:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: '#/components/schemas/AuditEntry'
      allOf:
        - $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowStatus'
    FlowSummary:
      type: object
      required:
        - flowId
        - definitionKey
        - currentState
        - status
        - terminalOutcome
        - documentRef
        - submitterId
        - updatedAt
      properties:
        flowId:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        definitionKey:
          type: string
        currentState:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/FlowRunStatus'
        terminalOutcome:
          type: string
          nullable: true
        documentRef:
          type: string
        submitterId:
          type: string
        updatedAt:
          type: string
          format: date-time
    SubmitDocumentRequest:
      type: object
      required:
        - definitionKey
        - documentRef
      properties:
        definitionKey:
          type: string
        documentRef:
          type: string
          description: Reference/identifier of the document
    TaskRunStatus:
      type: string
      enum:
        - PENDING
        - CLAIMED
        - COMPLETED
    TaskSummary:
      type: object
      required:
        - taskId
        - flowId
        - stateName
        - candidateGroupId
        - status
        - ownerId
      properties:
        taskId:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        flowId:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        stateName:
          type: string
        candidateGroupId:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: '#/components/schemas/TaskRunStatus'
        ownerId:
          type: string
          nullable: true
servers:
  - url: /api/v1
    description: Document Approval REST API
    variables: {}

content.text: Now update the CI workflow to add contracts steps before the UI steps:

content.content:
1	name: CI
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	    branches: [main]
8	
9	# Cancel any in-progress run for the same branch/PR when a new run starts.
10	# head_ref is the source branch name on PR events; ref_name is the branch on push events.
11	# Both resolve to the same value for a given branch, so push + PR don't double-run.
12	concurrency:
13	  group: ci-${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }}
14	  cancel-in-progress: true
15	
16	jobs:
17	  ci:
18	    name: Build, test & docs
19	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
20	    services:
21	      postgres:
22	        image: postgres:16-alpine
23	        env:
24	          POSTGRES_DB: wrkflw
25	          POSTGRES_USER: wrkflw
26	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: wrkflw
27	        ports:
28	          - 5432:5432
29	        options: >-
30	          --health-cmd pg_isready
31	          --health-interval 10s
32	          --health-timeout 5s
33	          --health-retries 5
34	    steps:
35	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
36	
37	      - name: Install tools (JDK 21 + uv)
38	        uses: jdx/mise-action@v2
39	        # Reads mise.toml; installs & caches temurin-21 + uv; trusts config.
40	
41	      - name: Cache Gradle packages
42	        uses: actions/cache@v4
43	        with:
44	          path: |
45	            ~/.gradle/caches
46	            ~/.gradle/wrapper
47	          key: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle.kts', 'gradle/libs.versions.toml') }}
48	          restore-keys: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-
49	
50	      - name: Cache uv packages
51	        uses: actions/cache@v4
52	        with:
53	          path: ~/.cache/uv
54	          key: uv-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('uv.lock') }}
55	          restore-keys: uv-${{ runner.os }}-
56	
57	      - name: Cache npm packages
58	        uses: actions/cache@v4
59	        with:
60	          path: ui/node_modules
61	          key: npm-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('ui/package-lock.json') }}
62	          restore-keys: npm-${{ runner.os }}-
63	
64	      - name: Run CI (lint + build + docs)
65	        run: mise run ci
66	
67	  docs-deploy:
68	    name: Publish docs to GitHub Pages
69	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
70	    needs: ci
71	    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
72	    permissions:
73	      pages: write
74	      id-token: write

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Cancel any in-progress run for the same branch/PR when a new run starts.
# head_ref is the source branch name on PR events; ref_name is the branch on push events.
# Both resolve to the same value for a given branch, so push + PR don't double-run.
concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  ci:
    name: Build, test & docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: wrkflw
          POSTGRES_USER: wrkflw
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: wrkflw
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install tools (JDK 21 + uv)
        uses: jdx/mise-action@v2
        # Reads mise.toml; installs & caches temurin-21 + uv; trusts config.

      - name: Cache Gradle packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.gradle/caches
            ~/.gradle/wrapper
          key: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle.kts', 'gradle/libs.versions.toml') }}
          restore-keys: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Cache uv packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/uv
          key: uv-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('uv.lock') }}
          restore-keys: uv-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Cache npm packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ui/node_modules
          key: npm-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('ui/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: npm-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Run CI (lint + build + docs)
        run: mise run ci

  docs-deploy:
    name: Publish docs to GitHub Pages
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: ci
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    permissions:
      pages: write
      id-token: write
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install tools
        uses: jdx/mise-action@v2

      - name: Cache Gradle packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.gradle/caches
            ~/.gradle/wrapper
          key: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle.kts', 'gradle/libs.versions.toml') }}
          restore-keys: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Cache uv packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/uv
          key: uv-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('uv.lock') }}
          restore-keys: uv-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Build docs
        run: mise run docs:build

      - name: Upload Pages artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: site/

      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deploy
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Cancel any in-progress run for the same branch/PR when a new run starts.
# head_ref is the source branch name on PR events; ref_name is the branch on push events.
# Both resolve to the same value for a given branch, so push + PR don't double-run.
concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  ci:
    name: Build, test & docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: wrkflw
          POSTGRES_USER: wrkflw
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: wrkflw
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install tools (JDK 21 + uv)
        uses: jdx/mise-action@v2
        # Reads mise.toml; installs & caches temurin-21 + uv; trusts config.

      - name: Cache Gradle packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.gradle/caches
            ~/.gradle/wrapper
          key: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle.kts', 'gradle/libs.versions.toml') }}
          restore-keys: gradle-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Cache uv packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/uv
          key: uv-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('uv.lock') }}
          restore-keys: uv-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Cache npm packages
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ui/node_modules
          key: npm-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('ui/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: npm-${{ runner.os }}-

      - name: Run CI (lint + build + docs)
        run: mise run ci

  docs-deploy:
    name: Publish docs to GitHub Pages
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: ci
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    permissions:
      pages: write
      id-token: write
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Cache Gradle packages

          path: |

      - name: Build docs

      - name: Upload Pages artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3

      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

input.new_string:
### Contract-first API design — [TypeSpec](https://typespec.io)

TypeSpec is the source of truth for REST contracts. The chain is:

```
contracts/*.tsp  →  tsp compile  →  openapi.yaml  →  openapi-typescript  →  ui/src/api/types.ts
```

The `.tsp` source lives in `contracts/` and emits directly to `specs/001-document-approval-engine/contracts/openapi.yaml`. The OpenAPI YAML and generated TypeScript types are both committed so the docs site and UI can consume them without running the TypeSpec compiler. Running `mise run contracts:build` regenerates the OpenAPI; `mise run ui:generate-types` regenerates the TypeScript types.

### Token / cost optimization

```

The `.tsp` source lives in `contracts/` and emits directly to `specs/001-document-approval-engine/contracts/openapi.yaml`. toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Agentic Coding

This project was built almost entirely by AI agents — with a human steering direction, reviewing outputs, and intervening when agents got stuck. This page explains the tooling landscape and how the pieces fit together, so you can replicate or extend the approach locally.

## The Core Idea

Rather than prompting an AI to write code directly, the workflow is **spec-first**:

1. Write a precise business specification before touching code
2. Let the agent derive an implementation plan from the spec
3. Break the plan into discrete, dependency-ordered tasks
4. Execute tasks one at a time — with the agent doing the implementation and tests

The spec acts as a contract between you and the agent. When the agent drifts, you correct the spec, not the code. When a task fails, the spec tells you what "correct" actually means.

## Tools Used

### Orchestrating the workflow — [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)

Spec Kit is the backbone of the development lifecycle. It enforces a structured flow from constitution → specification → plan → tasks → implement, and keeps a project constitution that all agents must honour.

See the [Spec Kit workflow](spec-kit.md) page for the full breakdown.

### AI agents / harnesses

| Tool | Role |
|------|------|
| [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code) (Sonnet) | Primary agent — specs, planning, architecture decisions, review |
| [opencode](https://opencode.ai) + Qwen / Kimi | Secondary harness — implementation tasks, cost reduction |
| [Voltagent specialists](https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents) | Language/domain specialists pulled in for targeted review or research |

Planning and specifications were done with Claude Sonnet. Once a `tasks.md` existed, implementation tasks were handed off to Qwen or Kimi via opencode for cost efficiency.

### Token / cost optimization

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| [mem0](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0) | Persistent cross-session memory — agents recall prior decisions without re-explaining context |
| [ogham-mcp](https://github.com/cpolzer/ogham-mcp) | Compressed context retrieval — reduces tokens spent on large codebases |
| [jcodemunch](https://github.com/jgravelle/jcodemunch-mcp) | Code chunking MCP — feeds only relevant file sections to the agent |

### Observability

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| [codeburn](https://github.com/getagentseal/codeburn) | Local token / cost tracking per session |
| [Entire](https://entire.io) | Overall session observability (being explored) |

## Practical Hints for Local Use

- **Start with the constitution.** Before specifying anything, run `/speckit-constitution` and answer its questions. This gives every subsequent agent a shared set of non-negotiable rules.
- **Clarify before planning.** Use `/speckit-clarify` to surface ambiguities in the spec before the agent makes technology decisions. Fixing a spec is cheap; fixing a plan is not.
- **Hand off at `tasks.md`.** Once `tasks.md` exists, you can switch to a cheaper model/harness. The tasks are small enough that context is low and model quality matters less.
- **Don't skip local validation.** `./gradlew build` before every push. CI is a safety net, not a first reviewer (Principle VI of the constitution).
- **Use mem0.** Without cross-session memory, agents repeat mistakes. With it, they accumulate project knowledge across sessions.

content.text:
Done. Here's how TypeSpec fits into this project's flow:

```
contracts/*.tsp          ← you edit this (single source of truth)
       ↓ mise run contracts:build
openapi.yaml             ← generated, committed for docs + tooling
       ↓ mise run ui:generate-types
ui/src/api/types.ts      ← generated TypeScript types, committed
```

**How it fits in "normally":** TypeSpec sits at the top of the artifact chain. You define types and routes once in `.tsp`, then emit OpenAPI for documentation and client SDK generation. Downstream consumers (the Vue UI, the MkDocs contracts page, Postman, contract tests) all read the generated YAML — they never see the TypeSpec source. The Ktor server still has hand-written route handlers; TypeSpec doesn't generate server stubs for Ktor (it would if this were .NET or Spring).
